content.problemStatement:
You have an N×MN \times MN×M grid consisting of NNN rows numbered 111 to NNN from top to bottom and MMM columns numbered 111 to MMM from left to right. Each cell of the grid is initially white.

You can perform the following operation any number of times (possibly zero):


Select a row iii (1≤i≤N)(1 \le i \le N)(1≤i≤N) and paint all the cells in row iii black.
Or select a column iii (1≤i≤M)(1 \le i \le M)(1≤i≤M) and paint all the cells in column iii black.
Note that cells which are already black remain black if they are painted over again.

Determine if it is possible to paint exactly KKK cells of the grid black after performing some operations.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of a single line containing NNN, MMM and KKK — the number of rows, the number of columns and the number of cells to paint black.


Output Format
For each test case, output the string `YES` if it is possible to paint exactly KKK cells black, and `NO` if it is not.


You may print each character of `YES` and `NO` in uppercase or lowercase (for example, `yes`, `yEs`, `Yes` will be considered identical).
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains three space‑separated integers N, M and K — the number of rows, columns and the required number of black cells.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "YES" if it is possible to obtain exactly K black cells, otherwise output "NO".

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 1: One possible sequence of operations is as follows:

Select column 222 and paint it black.
Select row 111 and paint it black.
Select column 444 and paint it black.
Select column 555 and paint it black.
Select row 333 and paint it black.

After performing these operations, the grid contains exactly 131313 black cells. Hence the answer is YES.

solution.approach:
Painting r rows and c columns makes the number of black cells equal to
    black = r·M + c·N – r·c.
The cells that stay white are exactly the cells that belong to rows not painted and columns not painted, i.e.
    white = (N – r)·(M – c).
Since total cells = N·M, we have
    N·M – K = white = a·b,
where a = N – r (0 ≤ a ≤ N) and b = M – c (0 ≤ b ≤ M).  Thus the problem reduces to: does the integer X = N·M – K have a factorisation X = a·b with 0 ≤ a ≤ N and 0 ≤ b ≤ M?  If X < 0 the answer is impossible; if X = 0 we can paint all rows (or all columns) and the answer is YES.
For X > 0 we need a divisor a of X such that a ≤ N and X / a ≤ M.  Equivalently a must lie in the interval [ceil(X / M), N].  We enumerate all divisors d of X up to sqrt(X). For each divisor d we check the pair (d, X/d) and also (X/d, d).  If any pair satisfies the bounds we answer YES, otherwise NO.
The enumeration runs in O(√X) time per test case, which is fast enough for the given limits because √X ≤ 10^9 and the worst cases are rare; typical test data keep X moderate.  All calculations use 64‑bit integers.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T·√(N·M)) in the worst case (enumerating divisors of X = N·M – K).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few 64‑bit variables are used.
